**[41:27]** The city gets to keep a smaller amount to put towards the costs that are increasing to help keep the tax rate down. And actually, I guess maybe here I'll talk about that. So state revenue sharing, just quickly, because this is important, it's broken into two components, Rev 1 and Rev 2. Rev 1, essentially everybody gets. Rev 2 is only given to communities that have high mill rates, or it has to be at a certain amount or above. And for the past several years, to the surprise of some, I'm sure, South Portland's mill rate has been below that

**[42:15]** threshold. So we have gotten $0 in Rev 2, while other communities with higher tax rates have gotten higher amounts. This year, for the first time in several years, we barely squeaked over that mark and we qualified for Rev 2, and that's about $820,000. That's great news this year, but if next year our tax rate doesn't go up enough to whatever the state adjusts it to next year, we lose all of that. It's the difference between total city assets and liabilities. And a component of that fund balance is

**[43:01]** unassigned, which means there's been no use assigned to it, and there's no restrictions on those dollars. Our policy says that in our unassigned fund balance, we need to have between 9% to 12% of whatever the next year's budget is going to be. So if it's a $100 million budget, we need 9% to 12% of whatever that number is to be kept in fund balance. Any amounts over that 12% can be, and are, used for one-time expenditures such as capital improvement projects. So this year, as of June 30th of 2025, our unassigned fund balance stood at 23.8 million. That's 18.7% of the proposed budget expenditures. So that gave us a pretty big cushion between

**[43:49]** the 12% and the 18.7 to use for operating and capital budget purchases. So how did we use it this year? In our FY27 budget, we're using 250,000 towards the operating budget. Essentially what that does is it buys down the tax rate a little bit. We've been using less and less for that purpose over the past several years. So for example, that amount is 29% less than this current budget year and 74% less than it was in FY23. We're also using 300,000 for a one-time budget expense. That's some of the more appropriate uses of fund balance because it's a one-time use of funds, then next year there's no expense, and therefore there's no expectation that fund balance covers that expense.

**[44:37]** And then we were able to use just over $3 million towards our CIP. So that totaled just about $4 million. In addition, our finance director has set aside $3 million as an anticipated loan to the school department, due to an anticipated budget overage that they'll have to close out this current budget year. So there's overages that we're preparing for of about 2 million in their operating budget and about 1 million in their food service budget. So again, about 3 million. **[47:40]** So the amount we need to raise from taxes is a math formula. What are your expenses? Subtract your non-property tax revenue, and that gives you the total need from property taxes or your tax commitment. So you do that for the school, for the municipal budget, for the county assessment, and the metro assessment. And so all told there, you're looking at just over 138 million in expenses. There's about 41.6 million in non-property tax revenues, and that means you have 96.4 million in needs from property taxes, and that's a 5% increase from last year. **[48:29]** taxable value. So in South Portland's case, we're estimating just over 6.7 billion, and that gives you the 0.01434. You multiply that by 1,000, that gives you a mill rate of 1434. We then can, yes, track that vehicle's, where it is. We can send officers that are out to that area to search for that vehicle. "Hey, it just went by this camera. Anybody in the area?" "Yeah, I'm by the mall." "Okay, I'm going to look for it." Hopefully stop the vehicle and we go from there.

**[118:12]** So surveilling individuals, keeping information, yes. The information that we gather from these plate readers are kept for 21 days. That's main state law. That's law. That's what we do. **[118:59]** I'm sure there have been examples of that, and I think I've read some of those where officers have been disciplined and fired for that improper use of that, and I would fire an officer for improper use of this technology. If you think about a police officer who's out there and has their own internal biases, and they're like,

**[119:44]** "Well, I want to run that guy. I didn't like what I saw in that car." Right? "I don't like the look of that car. It's all beat up." There's some internal bias. These cameras have none of that. **[223:12]** who was making a request on your property. And you can do it through Cumberland County, or you can do it through... The site I used had a government address, which was the Register of Deeds in Portland. And you just go in, and it is a little funky in that when you sign up to do it, it gives all of the different counties and different states. So just scroll down to Cumberland. And you don't have to know what your property number, again, it's done by name. And so you will be alerted with an email, and also sometimes a letter. I did an email, so that if there's any action on any of your parcels, your property,

**[223:57]** you will be alerted. That doesn't mean it's going to have a good outcome, but the quicker you know, the better your chances that you can put a stop to what's happening. And so yeah, do that for yourself, for your title of deed to be secure. And if anything, if another party attempts to buy property, sell property that is of yours, you'll be alerted. Thank you.
